topics_flat: Algebra > Equations and Inequalities > Linear and quadratic inequalities
prompt:
For any positive integer $N$ we denote by $\overline\{N\}$ the number obtained by writing the digits of $N$ from right to left. For instance, if $N = 2395$ then $\overline\{N\} = 5932$ and if $N = 3780$ then $\overline\{N\} = 873$.
Find all positive integers $N$ such that $N - \overline\{N\} = 9$.
response_text:
Let \(R(N)=\overline{\{N\}}\) be the number obtained by reversing the digits of \(N\) and deleting leading zeros if they appear after reversal.

We want
\[
N-R(N)=9.
\]

We will show that the only possibilities are the two-digit numbers
\[
10,21,32,43,54,65,76,87,98.
\]

## 1) First, one-digit numbers are impossible
If \(N\) has one digit, then \(R(N)=N\), so \(N-R(N)=0\neq 9\).

So \(N\) has at least two digits.

## 2) Two-digit numbers
Let
\[
N=10a+b \qquad (1\le a\le 9,\ 0\le b\le 9).
\]
Then
\[
R(N)=10b+a,
\]
except when \(b=0\), in which case \(R(N)=a\); but the same computation below still works.

Now
\[
N-R(N)=(10a+b)-(10b+a)=9(a-b).
\]
We want this to equal \(9\), so
\[
a-b=1.
\]
Hence all two-digit solutions are
\[
10,21,32,43,54,65,76,87,98.
\]

## 3) No solution with at least 3 digits
Let \(N\) have \(k\ge 3\) digits:
\[
N=\overline{a_k a_{k-1}\dots a_1},
\]
with \(a_k\neq 0\). Then
\[
R(N)=\overline{a_1 a_2\dots a_k}.
\]

A standard fact: for any integer, the number and the sum of its digits are congruent modulo \(9\). Reversing digits does not change the digit sum, so
\[
N\equiv R(N)\pmod 9.
\]
Thus \(N-R(N)\) is divisible by \(9\), so getting \(9\) is at least possible modulo \(9\); but we need more.

Now compare the sizes. Since \(k\ge 3\), changing the first and last digits already affects the value by a multiple of \(10^{k-1}-1\), which is at least \(99\). More concretely,
\[
N-R(N)
= (a_k-a_1)(10^{k-1}-1)+(a_{k-1}-a_2)(10^{k-2}-10)+\cdots.
\]
Each coefficient is divisible by \(9\), and for \(k\ge 3\) the smallest nonzero absolute value that such an expression can take is at least \(99-90=9\), but in fact equality \(9\) cannot occur because the first term is a multiple of \(99\), and all remaining terms are multiples of \(90,900,\dots\). Therefore the whole difference is a multiple of \(9\) and, for \(k\ge 3\), actually a multiple of \(90\) or differs from such by at least \(99\), so it cannot be exactly \(9\).

A cleaner way is to look modulo \(10\):  
Since \(N-R(N)=9\), we have
\[
N\equiv R(N)-1 \pmod{10}.
\]
But \(N\pmod{10}\) is the last digit \(a_1\), while \(R(N)\pmod{10}\) is the first digit \(a_k\). So
\[
a_1\equiv a_k-1\pmod{10}.
\]
Since digits are between \(0\) and \(9\), this means \(a_k=a_1+1\).

Now subtract:
\[
N-R(N)=(a_k-a_1)(10^{k-1}-1)+\text{terms divisible by }90.
\]
Because \(a_k-a_1=1\), the first term is \(10^{k-1}-1\), which is at least \(99\) for \(k\ge3\). Adding/subtracting multiples of \(90\) can never reduce this to \(9\), since
\[
10^{k-1}-1\equiv -1\pmod{90},
\]
so the whole expression would be congruent to \(-1\equiv 89\pmod{90}\), not \(9\). Contradiction.

Hence no \(k\ge3\)-digit solution exists.

So only the two-digit solutions remain.

Final answer: \(N\in\{10,21,32,43,54,65,76,87,98\}\).
